step.title: Step 11
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> SPACE

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 21 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 33, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 has a blue background with a light blue vertical divider strip, a yellow right-edge strip, and a black bottom bar. Three "F"-like shapes exist: (1) a black F-shape on the left containing 5 white dot children, (2) a charcoal F-shape on the right, and (3) a yellow F-shape at bottom-right. The white dots appear to be interactive elements. No clear player avatar visible.
- Goal model: Click on the F-shapes or white dots with MOUSE before the timer runs out. The white dots inside the black F-shape are likely targets.
- Action model: Arrow keys, SPACE, MOUSE, and ACTION7 available. Need to probe what they do.
- Plan: Move cursor left with many LEFT presses, then SPACE, to see if a mark appears away from the timer strip.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 32 of about 32 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Key finding! The black F-shape (node 5) has MOVED! 
Before (from earlier turns):
- Black F-shape boundary was around [[15, 15], [15, 23], [23, 23], [23, 21]]
- White dots were at (16, 19), (16, 22), (16, 25), (19, 25), (22, 25)
After the LEFT x20 + SPACE batch:
- Black F-shape boundary is now [[15, 0], [15, 8], [23, 8], [23, 6], [18, 6], [17, 5], [17, 0]]
- White dots are now at (16, 1), (16, 4), (16, 7), (19, 7), (22, 7)
The F-shape moved from column ~15-25 to column 0-8. That's a shift of about 15 columns to the left!
Also, the charcoal F-shape (node 6) moved from column ~36-44 to column 54-62. That's a shift to the right!
So LEFT arrow keys move the F-shapes! The black F-shape moved left, and the charcoal F-shape moved right (relative to the divider, they're moving apart).
Wait, let me reconsider. The black F-shape was at columns 15-25 and is now at columns 0-8. That's 15 columns left. The charcoal F-shape was at columns 36-44 and is now at 54-62. That's 18 columns right.
So pressing LEFT moves the left F-shape to the left and the right F-shape to the right? That's interesting. Or maybe LEFT moves everything on the left side left, and everything on the right side right (they mirror each other)?
Actually wait, looking at the yellow F-shape... it's still at the same position (around row 45, col 51). So only the black and charcoal F-shapes moved.
Let me also check: the timer went from 6 to 13 pixels. So 21 actions (20 LEFT + 1 SPACE) added 7 timer pixels. That means SPACE added 1 pixel and LEFT added... 6 pixels from 20 LEFT presses? That's about 0.3 pixels per LEFT. Hmm, maybe the timer only advances with SPACE and some arrow keys?
Actually, let me reconsider the timer. Maybe the timer advances based on how far the objects move, not based on the number of actions. Or maybe the timer is simply counting total "steps" and each action type advances it differently.
Wait, I need to look at this more carefully. Let me trace through:
- Initial: no timer (0 pixels)
- SPACE: timer = 1
- DOWN: timer = 2  
- LEFT: timer = 3
- UP: timer = 4
- RIGHT: timer = 5
- MOUSE x4: no change (timer still 5)
- SPACE: timer = 6
- LEFT x20 + SPACE: timer = 13
So from SPACE (6) to LEFT x20 + SPACE (13), that's 7 pixels from 21 actions. But earlier, each action added 1 pixel. So maybe the timer growth rate changed? Or maybe I'm miscounting.
Actually, the important discovery is that LEFT moves the F-shapes! This is the key mechanic. The game is about moving the F-shapes using arrow keys.
Now I need to figure out:
1. What the goal is (maybe align the F-shapes somehow?)
2. How the movement works (LEFT moves black F left, what does RIGHT do?)
3. What SPACE does (starts timer? places something?)
Let me think about the goal. The F-shapes are on opposite sides of a divider. Maybe the goal is to move them to specific positions, or to make them touch the divider, or to align them with each other.
Looking at the current state:
- Black F-shape is at columns 0-8 (left edge)
- Charcoal F-shape is at columns 54-62 (right edge, near the timer strip)
- Yellow F-shape is at columns 51-59, rows 45-47
The charcoal F-shape is now very close to the right edge. Maybe the goal is to move the F-shapes to the edges?
Let me try pressing RIGHT to see if it moves the F-shapes back.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: CRITICAL DISCOVERY! The LEFT arrow keys move the F-shapes! The black F-shape moved from columns 15-25 to columns 0-8 (15 columns left), and the charcoal F-shape moved from columns 36-44 to columns 54-62 (18 columns right). The arrow keys control F-shape movement!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check where the F-shapes are now and test RIGHT to reverse movement
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['B', 'c'] and n['pixels'] > 20:
        print(f"{n['color']} F-shape: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try RIGHT to see if it moves them back
predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT should move F-shapes rightward')
action(['RIGHT'])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter RIGHT:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['B', 'c'] and n['pixels'] > 20:
        print(f"{n['color']} F-shape: boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
